questions.stem: Which of the following is OUTSIDE the scope of AS 15?
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U1 — scope of AS 15

options.text: Benefits provided to casual and temporary staff without written contracts
options.explanation: AS 15 reaches all kinds of employees — full-time, part-time, permanent, casual or temporary.

options.text: Free housing and a car provided to a whole-time director
options.explanation: Non-monetary benefits count, and whole-time directors are expressly included as employees.

options.text: A pension paid to the legal heir of a deceased employee
options.explanation: Benefits for dependents, paid to heirs or nominees, are squarely inside the standard.

options.text: Benefits settled by granting the employer's own shares under an employee stock option plan
options.explanation: Employee share-based payments are carved out of AS 15 — they follow the ICAI Guidance Note instead.

questions.stem: Under AS 15, gratuity and pension are classified as:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U1 — four categories

options.text: Other long-term employee benefits
options.explanation: That family holds sabbaticals, jubilee awards and deferred bonuses — not retirement benefits.

options.text: Short-term employee benefits
options.explanation: Short-term benefits fall due wholly within twelve months of the period of service — not these.

options.text: Termination benefits
options.explanation: Termination benefits flow from ENDING employment early, not from completing it.

options.text: Post-employment benefits
options.explanation: Both fall due after employment ends — the defining mark of post-employment benefits.

questions.stem: An enterprise outsources its canteen to a caterer. Which single feature would MOST strongly pull the arrangement inside AS 15?
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U1 — contract of vs for services

options.text: The caterer's staff are obliged to obey the enterprise's orders as to the work, its mode and manner of performance
options.explanation: That is the hallmark of a contract OF service — an employment relationship in substance.

options.text: The caterer renders professional services subject to limited direction and control
options.explanation: Limited direction points to a contract FOR services — outside the employment relationship.

options.text: The caterer raises a monthly invoice with GST
options.explanation: Billing mechanics say nothing about the substance of the relationship.

options.text: The contract is renewed annually
options.explanation: Duration of a commercial contract does not convert a vendor into an employee.

questions.stem: For years an enterprise has paid every employee a festival lump sum, and withdrawal would badly damage employee relations. No plan or law requires it. Under AS 15 the enterprise should:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U1 — informal practices

options.text: Ignore it until a formal plan or legislation creates the obligation
options.explanation: AS 15 expressly covers informal practices, not just formal plans and statutes.

options.text: Provide for the benefit — the established pattern leaves it no realistic alternative but to pay
options.explanation: Informal practices that create an obligation with no realistic alternative are employee benefits under the standard.

options.text: Recognise the cost only in the year of actual payment
options.explanation: The obligation builds with service rendered — cash timing does not govern recognition.

options.text: Disclose it as a contingent liability under AS 29
options.explanation: There is nothing contingent here: the pattern makes the obligation effectively certain.

questions.stem: Salary cost for 20X1–X2 is ₹288 lakh; ₹266 lakh is paid by 31 March 20X2 and the rest in April 20X2. For 20X1–X2 the enterprise recognises:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U1 — short-term benefits recognition

options.text: Expense ₹288 lakh; liability ₹22 lakh
options.explanation: The undiscounted amount for service rendered is the expense; the unpaid gap is a liability.

options.text: Expense ₹266 lakh; contingent liability ₹22 lakh
options.explanation: There is nothing uncertain about salary already earned — it is a plain liability.

options.text: Expense ₹288 lakh; prepayment ₹22 lakh
options.explanation: A prepayment arises when cash paid EXCEEDS the expense, not when it falls short.

options.text: Expense ₹266 lakh; nothing further
options.explanation: That is cash accounting — the service was rendered in full, so the full cost accrues.

questions.stem: 80 employees each earn 6 working days of leave a year, carried forward one year. At the year end, experience says 70 employees will stay within next year's entitlement and 10 will exceed it by an average of 1.8 days each, drawing on the carried balance. A day of pay costs ₹2,000. The year-end leave liability is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U1 — accumulating compensated absences

options.explanation: That prices 10 employees' full 6-day entitlement — the liability is only the expected excess days.
Only the EXTRA payments expected from accumulated unused entitlement count: 10 × 1.8 = 18 days × ₹2,000.
That is every employee's full annual entitlement — next year's fresh entitlement is next year's cost.
That assumes 10 employees take one extra day each; the estimate is 1.8 extra days each.

questions.stem: Maternity leave that neither carries forward nor converts to cash is recognised:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U1 — non-accumulating absences

options.text: Only by way of a note to the accounts
options.explanation: When the absence occurs there is a real expense, not a disclosure item.

options.text: Rateably over each employee's expected service life
options.explanation: Spreading applies to benefits earned by service; this benefit is not earned by accumulation.

options.text: As an expense when the absence occurs — no liability accrues in advance
options.explanation: Non-accumulating absences are not linked to past service, so no obligation builds before the leave is taken.

options.text: As a liability measured at each balance sheet date
options.explanation: That is the treatment for ACCUMULATING absences with unused entitlement.

questions.stem: Accumulated leave lapses if an employee resigns (non-vesting). The correct treatment of the unused balance at the year end is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U1 — vesting vs non-vesting

options.text: Recognise the expected cost, factoring in the probability that some employees leave before using it
options.explanation: Non-vesting entitlements follow the same recognition rule; leaving probability enters the MEASUREMENT.

options.text: Recognise nothing, since employees receive no cash on leaving
options.explanation: Encashability is irrelevant to recognition — the absence itself will cost the employer pay.

options.text: Recognise the full undiscounted cost with no adjustment for departures
options.explanation: Measurement must reflect the possibility that employees leave before the leave is used.

options.text: Recognise the cost only when leave is actually availed
options.explanation: That ignores the obligation that built up as the entitlement was earned.

questions.stem: Employees hold 200 days of accumulated earned leave each with unlimited carry-forward, but company rules permit encashing or availing at most 40 days in the next twelve months. The benefit is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U1 — falls due + expected to occur

options.text: A post-employment benefit, because most of it will be encashed at retirement
options.explanation: Leave during service is not a retirement benefit; the long-term family covers it.

options.text: A short-term benefit, because each year's fresh entitlement arises annually
options.explanation: Classification looks at when the accumulated benefit falls due and is expected to be used — not at how it accrues.

options.text: A long-term benefit — it has not fallen due wholly within twelve months, so it is measured with discounting
options.explanation: Restrictions on encashment/availment mean the benefit has not fallen due; it shifts to the other-long-term rules.

options.text: A termination benefit to the extent encashed on resignation
options.explanation: Termination benefits flow from ending employment early — not from leave entitlements.

questions.stem: A plan pays employees 3% of net profit (₹1,80,00,000) if they serve the full year; expected staff turnover trims the eventual payout to 2.5% of net profit. The year's expense is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U1 — profit-sharing and bonus plans

options.text: Nil — profit-sharing is an appropriation of profit, not an expense
options.explanation: AS 15 treats profit-sharing for service rendered as an EXPENSE, not a distribution.
That is only the 0.5% turnover saving — the payment itself is the obligation, not the saving.
Measure the amount expected to be paid: 2.5% × ₹1,80,00,000.
That books the full 3% and ignores expected turnover — the estimate must be realistic.

questions.stem: In a defined contribution plan, actuarial risk and investment risk fall on:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U1 — defined contribution vs defined benefit

options.text: The employee — the employer's obligation ends with the fixed contribution
options.explanation: Once the fixed contribution is paid, benefit shortfalls and poor fund returns are the employee's problem.

options.text: The employer and employee equally, by convention
options.explanation: AS 15 knows no such sharing convention — the plan's design allocates the risk wholly.

options.text: The employer, who must top up any shortfall in benefits
options.explanation: That describes a DEFINED BENEFIT plan — the promise of an outcome keeps both risks with the employer.

options.text: The fund manager administering the plan
options.explanation: The manager runs the fund; the economic risk sits with whoever bears benefit shortfalls.

questions.stem: An enterprise funds its gratuity scheme through insurance premiums, but must pay employees directly if the insurer defaults. The plan is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U1 — insured benefits test

options.text: Outside AS 15, being an insurance contract
options.explanation: The insurance contract is merely the funding route for an employee benefit inside AS 15.

options.text: A state plan administered by the insurer
options.explanation: State plans are established by legislation and operated by government — not this.

options.text: A defined contribution plan, because premiums are fixed
options.explanation: Fixed premiums are not the test — the residual obligation to employees is.

options.text: A defined benefit plan — the residual obligation stays with the enterprise
options.explanation: Insurance is DC only when the enterprise has NO obligation to pay benefits directly or top up the insurer.

questions.topic: Ch 6 · AS 15 · Plan assets vs reimbursement rights
questions.stem: Fund balances that qualify as PLAN ASSETS, versus balances that are only REIMBURSEMENT RIGHTS, differ in the balance sheet because:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U1 — plan assets vs reimbursement rights

options.text: Plan assets are carried at cost while reimbursement rights are at fair value
options.explanation: Both are measured on a fair-value basis; measurement is not the difference.

options.text: Reimbursement rights are netted against the obligation; plan assets are shown separately
options.explanation: Exactly backwards — plan assets net, reimbursement rights stay gross.

options.text: Only plan assets may exceed the obligation in the balance sheet
options.explanation: The asset-ceiling question is separate; the distinction here is net versus gross presentation.

options.text: Plan assets are netted against the defined benefit obligation; reimbursement rights are shown gross as a separate asset
options.explanation: Both are measured at fair value — but netting versus gross presentation changes how heavy the balance sheet looks.

questions.stem: At the year end: present value of the defined benefit obligation ₹16,80,000; past service cost not yet recognised ₹80,000; fair value of plan assets ₹11,50,000. The balance-sheet liability is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U1 — defined benefit liability formula

options.explanation: That ADDS the unrecognised past service cost instead of deducting it.
That skips the deduction for past service cost not yet recognised.
PV of obligation − unrecognised past service cost − plan assets: 16,80,000 − 80,000 − 11,50,000.
That deducts only the past service cost and forgets the plan assets held to settle the obligation.

questions.stem: The defined benefit computation yields a NEGATIVE net amount (surplus) of ₹2,40,000. The present value of refunds and reductions in future contributions available from the plan is ₹1,50,000. The asset recognised is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U1 — asset ceiling

options.explanation: The ceiling picks the lower figure; nothing in the standard nets the two.
A surplus is capped at what the enterprise can actually extract — refunds or contribution holidays.

options.text: Nil — a defined benefit surplus is never recognised as an asset
options.explanation: A surplus IS recognised, but only up to the economic benefits available.
The asset is the LOWER of the computed surplus and the present value of available refunds/contribution reductions.

questions.stem: Plan assets open at ₹6,00,000; the expected return is 8% a year (4% for a half-year). On 30 September, contributions of ₹1,20,000 come in and benefits of ₹40,000 are paid out. The expected return on plan assets for the year is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U1 — expected return on plan assets

options.explanation: That is only the half-year return on the net inflow, dropping the opening assets' return.
8% on ₹6,00,000 for the full year (₹48,000) + 4% on the net ₹80,000 inflow held six months (₹3,200).
That ignores the return earned on the mid-year net inflow of ₹80,000.
That gives the mid-year inflow a FULL year's return of 8% — it was held only six months.

questions.stem: Plan assets: opening fair value ₹7,20,000, closing ₹9,80,000. During the year the employer contributed ₹2,10,000 and the plan paid benefits of ₹1,60,000. The ACTUAL return on plan assets is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U1 — actual return on plan assets

options.explanation: That removes contributions but forgets to add back the benefits the fund paid out.
That adds contributions and deducts benefits — both adjustments are backwards.
Closing − opening − contributions + benefits paid: 9,80,000 − 7,20,000 − 2,10,000 + 1,60,000.
That is just closing minus opening — contributions and benefit payments must be stripped out.

questions.topic: Ch 6 · AS 15 · Actuarial gains and losses
questions.stem: Under AS 15, actuarial gains and losses on a defined benefit plan are:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U1 — actuarial gains and losses

options.text: Deferred until settlement of the related obligation
options.explanation: Waiting for settlement would smooth results across years — precisely what the rule prevents.

options.text: Recognised immediately in the statement of profit and loss
options.explanation: No corridor, no spreading, no reserve routing — the full amount is income or expense of the year.

options.text: Recognised directly in reserves
options.explanation: Indian AS has no OCI/reserve route for actuarial remeasurements (contrast Ind AS 19).

options.text: Amortised over the average remaining working lives of employees
options.explanation: AS 15 (Revised) rejects spreading — recognition is immediate.

questions.stem: Match the events: (1) an employer pays the commuted value of pensions, ending its obligation for those benefits; (2) a segment is discontinued and, under a union agreement, its employees earn no FURTHER benefits though past entitlements stand.
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U1 — settlements and curtailments

options.text: Both are curtailments
options.explanation: In (1) the obligation is extinguished by payment — that is a settlement.

options.text: (1) is a curtailment; (2) is a settlement
options.explanation: Reversed — paying out the commuted value settles; cutting future accrual curtails.

options.text: (1) is a settlement; (2) is a curtailment
options.explanation: Settlement ELIMINATES obligations; curtailment SHRINKS the plan — fewer employees covered or benefits reduced for future service.

options.text: Both are settlements
options.explanation: In (2) employees keep benefits for past service — nothing was settled, future accrual was cut.

questions.stem: A board resolves in principle to offer VRS next year; no scheme details are approved and no reliable estimate exists. Under AS 15 the enterprise:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U1 — termination benefits

options.text: Recognises nothing yet — a liability needs a duly approved detailed formal plan AND a reliable estimate
options.explanation: AS 15 demands crystallisation — e.g. an approved detailed scheme, with acceptance evidence — before termination cost is booked.

options.text: Provides its best guess of the scheme cost immediately
options.explanation: Without an approved detailed plan and reliable estimate, recognition criteria fail.

options.text: Recognises a liability because the board resolution is a past event
options.explanation: An in-principle resolution leaves the enterprise free to change course — no obligation has crystallised.

options.text: Discloses the resolution as a prior period item
options.explanation: Prior period items are errors/omissions of earlier years under AS 5 — irrelevant here.

questions.stem: A VRS liability of ₹6,00,000 (reliably estimated, plan approved) falls due two years after the balance sheet date. The appropriate discount factor is 0.857. The amount recognised is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U1 — termination benefits measurement

options.explanation: Undiscounted recognition is only for benefits falling due within twelve months.

options.text: Nil, until the employees actually retire
options.explanation: The approved plan and reliable estimate crystallise the liability now.
Termination cost is never spread over the payout period — it is recognised at once, discounted.
Termination benefits that fall due beyond twelve months from the balance sheet date are discounted: 6,00,000 × 0.857.

questions.stem: Under AS 29, a provision is best described as:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U2 — definitions

options.text: A reduction in the carrying amount of a doubtful asset
options.explanation: Depreciation and doubtful-debt 'provisions' adjust assets and live in other standards — AS 29 means liabilities.

options.text: An amount set aside out of profits to strengthen the financial position
options.explanation: Appropriations of profit are reserves — a provision is a liability, charged in arriving at profit.

options.text: A possible obligation whose existence future events will confirm
options.explanation: That is the first limb of a CONTINGENT liability, which is never recognised.

options.text: A liability which can be measured only by using a substantial degree of estimation
options.explanation: Uncertain amount or timing, estimated substantially — that is the defining feature of a provision.

questions.stem: Which of the following falls WITHIN AS 29?
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U2 — scope exclusions

options.text: Expected losses on a construction contract
options.explanation: AS 7 deals with construction contracts, and a specific standard displaces AS 29.

options.text: An insurer's liabilities under contracts with policy-holders
options.explanation: Policy-holder obligations of insurance enterprises are expressly scoped out.

options.text: Obligations under financial instruments carried at fair value
options.explanation: Fair-valued financial instruments are the first scope exclusion.

options.text: A provision for an executory contract that has become onerous
options.explanation: Executory contracts are excluded EXCEPT where the contract is onerous.

questions.stem: A provision is recognised when — and only when:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U2 — recognition conditions

options.text: A future operating loss is forecast reliably
options.explanation: Future operating losses meet neither the liability definition nor the recognition criteria.

options.text: Management judges that prudence warrants a charge against a good year's profits
options.explanation: Provisioning by prudence-of-mood is the profit smoothing AS 29 was written to stop.

options.text: A present obligation exists and an outflow is at least possible
options.explanation: A merely possible outflow yields a contingent liability disclosure, not a provision.

options.text: A present obligation from a past event exists, an outflow of resources is probable, and a reliable estimate can be made
options.explanation: All three limbs must hold; fail any one and no provision is recognised.

questions.stem: A customer sues for damages; on all available evidence (including counsel's opinion) it is MORE LIKELY THAN NOT that the enterprise is liable, and the amount is estimable, with payment probable. The enterprise should:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U2 — present obligation, evidence

options.text: Do nothing until the court decides
options.explanation: Waiting for certainty defeats the standard — probability, not finality, triggers recognition.

options.text: Disclose a contingent liability only
options.explanation: Disclosure-only is for possible obligations or improbable outflows — this one crossed both lines.

options.text: Recognise a provision
options.explanation: Existence of the obligation is judged on the balance of evidence — more likely than not, plus probable outflow and estimate → provide.

options.text: Recognise the amount directly against opening reserves
options.explanation: Provisions are charged in the statement of profit and loss, never smuggled through reserves.

questions.stem: A new law will require factories to fit smoke filters by next year; the enterprise has not fitted them by the balance sheet date. A provision for the cost of fitting filters is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U2 — obligating events and future conduct

options.text: Disclosed as a contingent asset
options.explanation: A future compliance cost could never be an asset of any kind.

options.text: Recognised, because the law makes the cost unavoidable
options.explanation: The cost of compliance is avoidable by changing how the enterprise operates — unlike a penalty for damage already done.

options.text: Recognised at a discounted amount
options.explanation: Discounting is beside the point — there is no present obligation to measure.

options.text: Not recognised — the enterprise can avoid the expenditure by its own future actions, so no present obligation exists
options.explanation: Only obligations existing INDEPENDENTLY of future conduct qualify; the filters can still be fitted, or operations changed.

questions.stem: An enterprise has contaminated land. No law currently requires a clean-up, but at the balance sheet date new legislation demanding rectification is VIRTUALLY CERTAIN to be enacted shortly. The enterprise should:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U2 — new legislation

options.text: Provide for the clean-up cost — the damage is the past event and the virtually certain law makes the obligation real
options.explanation: Causing the damage becomes an obligating event when legislation requiring rectification is virtually certain to be enacted.

options.text: Wait for the law to receive assent before providing
options.explanation: Virtual certainty of enactment is the recognition trigger — formal assent is not required.

options.text: Do nothing, as future legislation can never create a present obligation
options.explanation: Where a proposed law is virtually certain, it does exactly that for damage already caused.

options.text: Disclose a contingent liability, since the law is not yet in force
options.explanation: With enactment virtually certain and the outflow probable, the obligation has crystallised into a provision.

questions.stem: Each individual product warranty is unlikely to be claimed, but across all units sold some claims are clearly probable. The enterprise:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U2 — probable outflow, class of obligations

options.text: Discloses a contingent liability for the whole class
options.explanation: The class-level outflow IS probable — that pushes it past disclosure into recognition.

options.text: Provides nothing, since no single claim is probable
options.explanation: Item-by-item probability is the wrong lens for a population of similar obligations.

options.text: Provides for the class as a whole at its best estimate
options.explanation: Similar obligations are judged as a class — probability across the class satisfies the outflow test.

options.text: Provides only for claims actually lodged by the year end
options.explanation: The obligating event is the SALE with warranty, not the lodging of a claim.

questions.stem: An enterprise is pursuing a damages claim with a PROBABLE (but not virtually certain) inflow. Under AS 29 the claim is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U2 — contingent assets

options.text: Recognised as income on filing the claim
options.explanation: Recognition waits for VIRTUAL CERTAINTY, when the asset stops being contingent at all.

options.text: Recognised, with an equal provision for uncertainty
options.explanation: Grossing up a hoped-for gain with a fake provision recognises income that may never arrive.

options.text: Not recognised and not disclosed in the financial statements — it may be described in the report of the approving authority
options.explanation: Contingent assets stay out of the books AND the notes; a probable inflow surfaces only in the Board's report.

options.text: Disclosed in the notes to the financial statements
options.explanation: The notes route belongs to contingent LIABILITIES — a favourite trap.

questions.topic: Ch 6 · AS 29 · Joint and several liability
questions.stem: An enterprise is jointly and severally liable for ₹10,00,000; co-obligors are expected to meet ₹6,00,000 of it and their ability to pay is not in doubt. The enterprise's own probable share is ₹4,00,000. Treatment:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U2 — joint and several liability

options.text: Provision ₹4,00,000; the ₹6,00,000 expected from co-obligors is a contingent liability
options.explanation: Provide for the slice the enterprise will probably settle; the part expected to be met by others is contingent.

options.text: Provision ₹10,00,000, with a receivable of ₹6,00,000
options.explanation: That is reimbursement accounting — here the co-obligors' share is not the enterprise's probable outflow at all.

options.text: Provision ₹6,00,000; contingent liability ₹4,00,000
options.explanation: The proportions are swapped — provide what the enterprise itself will probably pay.

options.text: Contingent liability ₹10,00,000 only
options.explanation: The enterprise's own ₹4,00,000 outflow is probable and estimable — that slice must be provided.

questions.stem: On discounting, AS 29 says the amount of a provision:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U2 — measurement, discounting exception

options.text: May be discounted whenever the effect is material
options.explanation: AS 29 confines discounting to the named exception — materiality does not widen it.

options.text: Is not discounted, except decommissioning/restoration-type liabilities recognised as part of the cost of PPE, discounted at a pre-tax rate with the unwinding charged to the P&L
options.explanation: The general rule is undiscounted best estimate; the carve-out covers liabilities capitalised into PPE cost.

options.text: Is never discounted under any circumstances
options.explanation: The decommissioning/restoration exception is carved into the standard itself.

options.text: Is always discounted to present value at a post-tax rate
options.explanation: Neither limb is right — discounting is the exception, and the rate prescribed is PRE-tax.

questions.stem: A warranty provision stood at ₹42,000 at the start of the year; no part of it was used during the year. At the year end the best estimate of the obligation is ₹36,000. The income statement effect for the year is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U2 — review and adjustment of provisions

options.text: A credit of ₹6,000
options.explanation: Provisions are reviewed each balance sheet date and adjusted to the current best estimate — here the estimate FELL.

options.text: A charge of ₹36,000
options.explanation: The year-end balance is ₹36,000; only the movement of ₹6,000 touches the income statement.

options.text: A charge of ₹6,000
options.explanation: The required provision went DOWN — the adjustment releases, not charges.

options.text: A credit of ₹42,000
options.explanation: Only the unneeded slice is released — ₹36,000 of obligation remains.

questions.stem: Six similar suits are outstanding. For each: 60% chance of winning; 30% chance of losing with damages ₹90,000; 10% chance of losing with damages ₹1,80,000 (each case a separate entity). The appropriate treatment is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U2 — contingent liability measurement for disclosure

options.text: Provide ₹2,70,000
options.explanation: With a 40% chance of losing each case, the outflow is not probable — recognition fails.

options.text: Disclose a contingent liability of ₹2,70,000 — the aggregate expected loss
options.explanation: Loss per case is not probable (40%), so no provision; disclosure at expected value: (27,000 + 18,000) × 6.

options.text: Disclose ₹10,80,000, the maximum possible loss
options.explanation: Disclosing every case at maximum damages is unrealistic — expected loss is the sensible measure.

options.text: Neither provide nor disclose
options.explanation: The possibility of outflow is well above remote — disclosure is required.

questions.stem: A claim of ₹6,00,000 is pending; counsel advises the loss is possible but NOT probable. Legal costs of ₹75,000 will be incurred irrespective of the outcome. Treatment:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U2 — provision vs contingent liability split

options.text: Provide ₹75,000 for legal costs; disclose the ₹6,00,000 claim as a contingent liability
options.explanation: The unavoidable legal costs are a present obligation; the not-probable claim is disclosure-only.

options.text: Provide ₹6,75,000 in full
options.explanation: Providing a not-probable claim overstates liabilities — only the unavoidable costs are provided.

options.text: Disclose ₹6,75,000 as a contingent liability
options.explanation: The legal costs will be incurred whatever happens — they are a provision, not a contingency.

options.text: Provide ₹6,00,000 and disclose ₹75,000
options.explanation: Backwards — the certain-ish element is the costs, the contingent element is the claim.

questions.stem: An enterprise provides ₹5,00,000 for a claim. Recovery of ₹3,20,000 from its insurer is virtually certain if it settles. In the financial statements:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U2 — reimbursements

options.text: Provision ₹5,00,000; the recovery is recognised when cash arrives
options.explanation: Virtual certainty is the recognition trigger for the asset — not receipt.

options.text: Provision ₹1,80,000 net of the recovery
options.explanation: Netting on the balance sheet is prohibited — the enterprise remains liable for the whole ₹5,00,000.

options.text: Provision ₹5,00,000 and an asset ₹5,00,000 anticipating full recovery
options.explanation: The asset cannot exceed the amount virtually certain — ₹3,20,000.

options.text: Provision ₹5,00,000 and a separate asset ₹3,20,000; the P&L expense may be shown net at ₹1,80,000
options.explanation: The reimbursement is a separate asset (never above the provision); net presentation is allowed only in the P&L.

questions.stem: A restructuring provision may include:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U2 — restructuring provisions

options.text: Only direct expenditures necessarily entailed by the restructuring and not associated with the ongoing activities of the enterprise
options.explanation: Both tests must be met — costs that serve the continuing business are excluded.

options.text: Marketing costs to reposition the business after the restructuring
options.explanation: Marketing relates to future conduct, not the restructuring obligation.

options.text: Identifiable operating losses expected up to the restructuring date
options.explanation: Future operating losses are never provided — even inside a restructuring plan.

options.text: Retraining costs for staff redeployed to continuing operations
options.explanation: Retraining continuing staff serves the future business — expressly excluded.

questions.topic: Ch 6 · AS 29 · Sale of an operation
questions.stem: Management has decided to sell a division and is negotiating with two buyers. For AS 29, an obligation for the sale arises:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U2 — binding sale agreement

options.text: Only when there is a binding sale agreement
options.explanation: Until signature the enterprise can change its mind — or will have to, if no acceptable buyer appears.

options.text: When negotiations reach an advanced stage
options.explanation: Advanced talks are still talks; only a binding agreement removes the realistic alternative.

options.text: When the board minutes record the decision to sell
options.explanation: An internal decision binds nobody — the enterprise remains free to reverse course.

options.text: When the intended sale is announced to the press
options.explanation: AS 29 pins the obligation for a sale to the binding agreement, not to publicity.

questions.topic: Ch 6 · AS 15 · Case: Meghna Engineering (plan assets)
questions.case: Meghna Engineering Ltd runs a funded pension plan. On 1 April 20X1 the fair value of plan assets was ₹4,00,000. On 30 September 20X1 the plan paid out benefits of ₹40,000 and received contributions of ₹1,00,000. On 31 March 20X2 the fair value of plan assets was ₹5,30,000. At the start of the year the company estimated the expected rate of return on plan assets at 10% per annum (take 5% for amounts held for six months).

questions.stem: The EXPECTED return on plan assets for 20X1–X2 is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U1 — expected return computation

options.explanation: That stops at the opening assets and ignores the mid-year net inflow of ₹60,000.
That gives the ₹60,000 net inflow a full year's 10% — it was in the fund only six months.
10% of the CLOSING fair value is not how expected return is built — it works from opening assets plus timed flows.
10% on ₹4,00,000 held all year (₹40,000) + 5% on the net ₹60,000 inflow held six months (₹3,000).

questions.stem: The ACTUAL return on plan assets for 20X1–X2 is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U1 — actual return computation

options.explanation: Closing minus opening alone — the ₹1,00,000 of contributions did not come from investment performance.
That strips contributions but forgets to add back the ₹40,000 of benefits the fund paid out.
Closing 5,30,000 − opening 4,00,000 − contributions 1,00,000 + benefits paid 40,000.
That adds back only half the flows — both contributions (out) and benefits (back) must be adjusted.

questions.stem: The actuarial gain or loss on plan assets for the year, recognised per AS 15, is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U1 — actuarial gain on plan assets

options.text: A loss of ₹27,000, recognised immediately in the P&L
options.explanation: Actual return EXCEEDED expected — the difference is a gain.

options.text: Nil — differences are deferred and offset against future contributions
options.explanation: Deferral is exactly the smoothing AS 15 (Revised) forbids.

options.text: A gain of ₹27,000, credited to reserves
options.explanation: AS 15 has no reserves route — actuarial differences hit the P&L at once.

options.text: A gain of ₹27,000, recognised immediately in the P&L
options.explanation: Actual ₹70,000 − expected ₹43,000; AS 15 takes it straight to profit and loss.

questions.topic: Ch 6 · AS 15 · Case: Vindhya Textiles (gratuity obligation)
questions.case: Vindhya Textiles Ltd inducts Ms Rao at the beginning of year 1 on annual emoluments of ₹10,00,000, expected to grow at 10% per annum (she will receive 4 yearly increments). The company pays a lump sum of 25% of the last drawn annual salary for each completed year of service to employees retiring after a minimum 4-year term, which Ms Rao is expected to complete before retiring at the end of year 4. Discount rate 8%; PV factors: 0.926 (1 year), 0.857 (2 years), 0.794 (3 years).

questions.stem: The defined benefit obligation payable at retirement is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U1 — defined benefit obligation build-up

options.explanation: That gives a fifth increment; service ends after year 4.
That applies 25% × 4 to the CURRENT salary, ignoring four years of 10% growth.
Last drawn salary 10,00,000 × 1.1⁴ = 14,64,100; benefit = 14,64,100 × 25% × 4 years — which equals the final salary itself.
That grows the salary only three times — she receives 4 yearly increments.

questions.stem: The amount attributed to each year of service (before discounting) is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U1 — equal attribution of benefit

options.explanation: That divides by 5 years — the benefit is earned over 4 completed years.
That is 25% of the CURRENT salary — the benefit formula rides on the final salary.
The obligation of ₹14,64,100 is spread equally over the 4 years of service that earn it.
Charging everything to one year defeats the matching the standard demands.

questions.stem: The CURRENT SERVICE COST for year 2 (payment falls due at the end of year 4) is closest to:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U1 — current service cost discounting

options.explanation: Year 2's slice discounts 2 years to retirement: 3,66,025 × 0.857.
Undiscounted — but the payment is still two years away at the end of year 2.
That uses the 3-year factor (0.794), which belongs to year 1.
That uses the 1-year factor (0.926), which belongs to year 3.

questions.topic: Ch 6 · AS 29 · Case: Kaveri Appliances (warranty provision)
questions.case: Kaveri Appliances Ltd gives a two-year warranty and, from experience, provides on invoice value at 2% where LESS than one year of warranty remains and 3% where MORE than one year remains. Invoices raised: ₹80,000 on 12 February 20X0; ₹50,000 on 20 December 20X0; ₹1,60,000 on 6 October 20X1. The accounting year ends 31 March.

questions.stem: The warranty provision as at 31 March 20X1 is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U2 — warranty provision computation

options.explanation: Feb-20X0 sale has under a year of cover left (80,000 × 2% = 1,600); Dec-20X0 sale has more than a year (50,000 × 3% = 1,500).
Both invoices at 3% — the February 20X0 sale is into its final warranty year.
The rates are crossed: 3% belongs to the sale with MORE warranty left, 2% to the older one.
Both invoices at 2% — but the December sale still carries more than a year of warranty.

questions.stem: The warranty provision as at 31 March 20X2 is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U2 — warranty provision roll-forward

options.explanation: The rates are crossed between the December and October invoices.
That holds the December sale at 3% — by 31 March 20X2 it has slipped under a year of cover.
Feb-20X0's warranty has EXPIRED; Dec-20X0 now has under a year (50,000 × 2% = 1,000); Oct-20X1 has more than a year (1,60,000 × 3% = 4,800).
That still provides 2% on the February 20X0 sale — its two-year warranty ended in February 20X2.

questions.stem: The amount debited to the P&L for the year ended 31 March 20X2 is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U2 — provision movement to P&L

options.explanation: Adding the two balance-sheet figures has no meaning in the P&L.
That charges the full closing provision, double-counting the ₹3,100 already provided.
Provision required 5,800 − opening balance 3,100 — only the movement is charged.
That is only the provision on the October 20X1 invoice, ignoring movements on the older sales.

questions.case: Periyar Retail Ltd signs a binding agreement with its workers' union to close its wholesale division; the closure plan is detailed, approved and has begun. Estimated amounts: severance payments to division staff ₹15,00,000; retraining costs for staff redeployed to continuing stores ₹4,00,000; marketing for the new store format ₹2,50,000; new distribution software ₹3,00,000; operating losses expected up to the closure date ₹5,00,000; expected gain on selling the division's fixtures ₹1,20,000.

questions.stem: The restructuring provision recognised is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U2 — restructuring provision contents

options.explanation: Retraining redeployed staff serves the CONTINUING business — excluded.
Marketing and new systems relate to future conduct — both are excluded, as is retraining.
Future operating losses are never provided, and disposal gains are never netted against the provision.
Only severance is both necessarily entailed by the restructuring AND unconnected with continuing activities.

questions.stem: The expected ₹1,20,000 gain on selling the fixtures is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U2 — expected disposal gains

options.text: Ignored in measuring the provision and recognised on disposal under the standard governing the asset
options.explanation: Gains on expected disposals are never taken into account, however closely linked to the restructuring.

options.text: Recognised as income now, being part of an approved plan
options.explanation: An approved plan does not realise a gain — the disposal does.

options.text: Netted against the restructuring provision
options.explanation: Netting anticipated gains understates the provision — expressly prohibited.

options.text: Disclosed as a contingent liability
options.explanation: An expected GAIN could hardly be a contingent liability.

questions.stem: The ₹5,00,000 of operating losses expected up to the closure date are:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 6 U2 — future operating losses

options.text: Provided net of the expected fixtures gain
options.explanation: Two prohibited items do not make one permitted one.

options.text: Added to the restructuring provision
options.explanation: Even inside a restructuring, future operating losses fail the liability definition.

options.text: Not provided — future operating losses are recognised as they are incurred
options.explanation: Identifiable future operating losses up to a restructuring date are excluded from the provision.

options.text: Deferred as an asset and amortised after closure
options.explanation: Losses are never carried forward as assets.

questions.stem: State the objective of AS 15 and the four categories of employee benefits, with two examples of each. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Objective: recognise a liability when service is rendered in exchange for future benefits, and an expense when the benefit from service is consumed — cost matched to service period, not payment date
Short-term: wages/salaries, paid annual and sick leave, bonuses payable within 12 months, non-monetary perks
Post-employment: gratuity, pension, provident fund, post-employment medical care
Other long-term: sabbatical/long-service leave, jubilee awards, disability benefits, deferred bonuses beyond 12 months
Termination: retrenchment compensation, VRS payments; scope carve-out — employee share-based payments excluded

questions.stem: Classify compensated absences under AS 15 and state the recognition and measurement rules for each class. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Accumulating: carried forward for future use — recognise expense as employees render service that increases entitlement
Measurement: additional amount expected to be paid due to unused entitlement at the balance sheet date; undiscounted (short-term)
Vesting (encashable on leaving) vs non-vesting — same recognition; non-vesting measurement factors leaving probability
Non-accumulating (e.g. maternity leave): no carry-forward, no link to past service — recognise when the absence occurs

questions.stem: Distinguish defined contribution plans from defined benefit plans, indicating where actuarial and investment risk fall, and state when an insured plan is treated as defined benefit. (4 marks)

skeleton.point: DCP: fixed contributions to a separate fund, no further obligation; charge = period's contribution; e.g. provident fund
Risks in DCP fall on the EMPLOYEE (benefit may be less than expected; fund may underperform)
DBP: all other plans; employer bears actuarial and investment risk; charge from actuarial computation; e.g. gratuity
Insured plan = DCP unless the enterprise must pay benefits directly or top up the insurer's shortfall — then DBP

questions.stem: State the components of the defined benefit balance-sheet amount (including the asset ceiling) and the components of the P&L charge under AS 15. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Balance sheet: PV of defined benefit obligation − past service cost not yet recognised − fair value of plan assets
Negative net amount: recognise asset at LOWER of the amount computed and PV of refunds/reductions in future contributions
P&L: current service cost; interest cost; expected return on plan assets and reimbursement rights
P&L continued: actuarial gains/losses (immediate); past service cost recognised; curtailment/settlement effects; asset-ceiling effect

questions.stem: When are termination benefits recognised and how are they measured under AS 15? How is an uncertain VRS acceptance dealt with? (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Recognise only when a detailed formal plan is duly approved AND a reliable estimate exists — mere intention insufficient
Falling due within 12 months of the balance sheet date: undiscounted; beyond 12 months: discounted
Voluntary redundancy offers: measure by the number of employees expected to accept
Uncertainty over acceptances → contingent liability disclosed per AS 29; AS 5/AS 18 disclosures may also bite

questions.topic: Ch 6 · AS 29 · Recognition and the three-way sort
questions.stem: When is a provision recognised under AS 29? Contrast the treatments of a provision, a contingent liability and a contingent asset. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Provision: present obligation from past event + probable outflow (more likely than not) + reliable estimate — all three
Fail probability/estimate or only possible obligation → contingent liability: disclose unless the outflow possibility is remote
Contingent asset: never recognised, not disclosed in FS; report of the approving authority if inflow probable; recognise when virtually certain
Continual reassessment: contingent liability becomes a provision in the period the outflow turns probable

questions.stem: When may a restructuring provision be recognised, and which costs does it include and exclude? (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Restructuring: programme materially changing business scope or conduct (sale/termination of a line, closures/relocations, management-layer changes, fundamental reorganisations)
Recognise only on the general triple test; sale of an operation: no obligation until a BINDING sale agreement
Include only direct expenditures necessarily entailed AND not associated with ongoing activities
Exclude: retraining/relocating continuing staff, marketing, new systems and networks, future operating losses, expected disposal gains

questions.stem: State the measurement principles for provisions under AS 29, including the discounting rule and the treatment of reimbursements. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Best estimate of expenditure to settle the present obligation at the balance sheet date; management judgement + experience + expert reports; pre-tax
No discounting EXCEPT decommissioning/restoration-type liabilities capitalised into PPE cost — pre-tax market rate, unwinding to P&L
Risks/uncertainties and future events (existing technology, virtually certain legislation) reflected with objective evidence; no gains from expected disposals

Reimbursement: separate asset when receipt virtually certain, capped at the provision; P&L may present net; review provisions yearly, reverse when outflow no longer probable, use only for original purpose
